title: From national gaps to regional and global gaps
subtitle: A group’s Prosperity Gap is the average gap of all its members
source: Authors’ calculation using Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026).
visdescription: This chart shows national Prosperity Gaps aggregated to regional and global Prosperity Gaps.

scenes.text: In 2026, the Prosperity Gap for Colombia was 4.5 and for Peru, 3.5.
Each square represents an economy’s Prosperity Gap in 2026, colored by region and reflecting the population size.

There is a large difference in prosperity between economies. In some, such as the United States, Japan, and Estonia, incomes are almost high enough to meet the prosperity standard, but in others, such as South Sudan and Mozambique, incomes would have to grow roughly 25 times to reach that level.

The regional Prosperity Gaps reflect the average gap of all individuals living in the region. This is equivalent to taking the average Prosperity Gap across economies while accounting for the size of their population.

Today, most people in Europe & Central Asia and North America have reached the prosperity standard of $28 per day. On average, people in Latin America & the Caribbean need to increase their incomes about [emphasis: three times] to reach that same level of prosperity. In South Asia and the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan, incomes need to be about [emphasis: five times] higher, and in Sub-Saharan Africa, almost [emphasis: 11 times] higher.

The Global Prosperity Gap combines the Prosperity Gaps of every person in the world. Today, individual incomes worldwide would need to rise nearly [emphasis: five times] on average for everyone to reach the prosperity standard of $28 per day.
